beats.narration_text: Sawubona, Liam. This is Liam, in for Bear. The team didn't choose on-device AI — the radio's arithmetic left them nothing else to choose.

props.command: Should I run inference on the sensor or send images to the server over LoRaWAN?
output: One 20 KB image over LoRaWAN takes 640 seconds. Your airtime budget is 36 seconds per hour. Physics chose for you.

beats.narration_text: The book's four-constraint coupling diagram: memory, compute, power, real-time at the four corners. Every edge is a coupling — tighten one constraint and it pulls on two others.

One image. 20 kilobytes. That's 160,000 bits. LoRaWAN data rate zero: about 250 bits per second. Transmission time: 640 seconds — ten and a half minutes. Per image. Watch it crawl through the narrow pipe. The payload is physically too large for the channel. Not a software problem. Not a configuration problem. Physics.

The regulatory duty cycle: 1%. Your radio can transmit for 36 seconds per hour. One image needs 640 seconds. That's the ceiling slamming down. Your budget allows 0.056 images per hour. You need multiple per hour. The bar slams into the ceiling and stops. There is no optimized firmware that fixes this. The radio is the constraint.

Now send the classifier output instead. Ten bytes. Eighty bits. At 250 bits per second: 0.32 seconds. Same pipe, no problem. The on-device classifier runs, produces a class index and a confidence score — ten bytes total. That zips through in less than half a second. Thirty-six seconds of airtime budget now buys you over 100 classifications per hour. Physics chose the architecture.

Your turn. Ask Claude: I have a sensor that generates open bracket payload KB close bracket KB images at open bracket rate close bracket per hour. My radio is open bracket radio type close bracket with open bracket bps close bracket bits per second bandwidth and open bracket duty pct close bracket percent duty cycle. Walk me through: one — transmission time per image. Two — maximum images per hour given duty cycle. Three — whether on-device inference is forced by physics. Four — what my classifier output payload should be: class probabilities vs top-1 label vs a compact encoding.

props.command: I have a sensor that generates [payload_kb] KB images at [rate]/hour. My radio is [radio_type] with [bps] bps bandwidth and [duty_pct]% duty cycle. Walk me through: (1) transmission time per image, (2) maximum images per hour given duty cycle, (3) whether on-device inference is forced by physics, (4) what my classifier output payload should be (class probabilities vs top-1 label vs a compact encoding).
output: 20 KB × 8 = 160,000 bits ÷ 250 bps = 640s per image. Budget: 36s/hour. 10-byte result: 0.32s. Physics chose on-device inference.

props.title: The Architecture Physics Chose
props.subline: embedded ai · liam, in for bear
